coredata.dc:title: Determination of cholic acid in body fluids by β‑cyclodextrin-modified N-doped carbon dot fluorescent probes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Xia
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: An easy, dependable, and sensitive cholic acid activity experiment was designed based on β‑cyclodextrin-modified carbon dot (β‑CD-CD) nanoprobes with specific host–guest recognizing ability and photoelectron transfer capability. The β‑CD-CD nanoprobes were characterized by infrared, ultraviolet-visible, and fluorescence spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy. The fluorescence of the probes under optimized conditions linearly responded to cholic acid concentration from 0 to 650 μmol·L−1 with a detection limit of 25 nmol·L−1. The probes also performed well in detecting cholic acid in serum and urine samples with an average recovery rate of 97.1%–103.4%. Thus, this study provides a reliable, rapid, and easy method of cholic acid detection in body fluids that can be potentially applied in medical studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
β‑cyclodextrin
Host–guest recognition
Photoinduced electron transfer
Cholic acid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of β‑CD-CD-Fc+ host–guest fluorescent probes
CA detection mechanism of the β‑CD-CD fluorescent probes
Selectivity, anti-interference, and stability of the β‑CD-CD-Fc+ fluorescent probe
CA detection by the fluorescent probe systems

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra and (B) UV–vis spectra of CDs, β‑CD, and β‑CD-CDs; emission spectrum of β‑CD-CDs under the largest excitation at 340 nm. TEM images of β‑CD-CDs in (C) 2 nm and (D) 50 nm.

(A) Fluorescent intensity of β‑CD-CDs versus Fc+ concentration (0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 900 μmol·L−1) dissolved in PBS (10 m mol·L−1, pH 7.4). (B) Time of Fluorescence quenching phase (Time 1) and Fluorescence recovery phase (Time 2).
Effects of (A) other bile acids for CA detection and (B) mixture containing CA (100 μmol·L−1) and foreign substances on the fluorescent probe system.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) calibration plot of the proposed fluorescence assay at different concentrations of CA. Inset: Enlarged images of calibration plot for (a) 0–1 μmol·L−1 CA and (b) 1–650 μmol·L−1 CA.
Recovery of CA detection in real samples (mean ± s; n = 3).

head.ce:title: Determination of cholic acid in body fluids by β‑cyclodextrin-modified N-doped carbon dot fluorescent probes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An easy, dependable, and sensitive cholic acid activity experiment was designed based on β‑cyclodextrin-modified carbon dot (β‑CD-CD) nanoprobes with specific host–guest recognizing ability and photoelectron transfer capability. The β‑CD-CD nanoprobes were characterized by infrared, ultraviolet-visible, and fluorescence spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy. The fluorescence of the probes under optimized conditions linearly responded to cholic acid concentration from 0 to 650 μmol·L−1 with a detection limit of 25 nmol·L−1. The probes also performed well in detecting cholic acid in serum and urine samples with an average recovery rate of 97.1%–103.4%. Thus, this study provides a reliable, rapid, and easy method of cholic acid detection in body fluids that can be potentially applied in medical studies.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important part of bile, bile acid is a major cholesterol-synthesized steroidal compound in the liver and a necessary substance for fat metabolism in the human body [1]. Abnormal bile acid concentration in the body directly or indirectly causes many diseases [2]. The concentration of cholic acid (CA), a major substance in the bile acid of humans, is usually below 10 μmol·L−1 in human serum [3,4] and accounts for 31% of total bile acid [5,6]. CA concentration is an efficient diagnostic indicator of hepatic and intestinal diseases [5,7,8], which are major killers in the medical field owing to their prevalence, persistence, and high mortality [9]. Thus, qualitative and quantitative CA analyses are important both clinically and medically. CA can be identified by high-performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS-MS) [10–12], liquid-phase micro-extraction [13], gas-phase chromatography [14], electrochemistry [15], molecular imprinting [1,3,16], polymer dispersed liquid crystal [17], enzyme-linked DNA [18], supercritical fluid chromatography [19], ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy [20], and solid-phase radioimmunoassay [21]. However, these methods are limited by the expensive equipment, complex operation, long time consumption, low sensitivity, and low selectivity. Thus, low-cost, convenient, and rapid CA detection methods are urgently needed.

In the past decade, quantum dots (QDs) have been widely applied in chemical and biological detection sensors owing to their small sizes and luminescent properties [22–24]. However, traditional QDs are limited by toxicity, low biocompatibility, complex preparation process, and severe environmental pollution. As a substitute of traditional QDs, carbon QDs (CDs) are novel zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials [25,26] with easy preparation, photostability, water-solubility, chemical inertia, photobleaching resistance, functionalizability, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, biocompatibility, and abundant electron-hole exciton pairs. They also have small sizes and luminescent properties similar to traditional QDs and fluorescent dyes [27,28]. Since their discovery in 2004 [29], CDs have raised increasing expectations [30,31] and shown promising applications in drug carriers, sensors, biological markers, biological imaging, and photoelectrochemistry [32–34]. However, the majority of directly obtained CDs are limited by low fluorescent quantum yields (FLQY <50%) and the consequent low detection sensitivity. To improve the FLQY of CDs, researchers usually perform surface passivation or element doping to adjust the chemical structure of CDs [32,35]. Nitrogen atom, a commonly used heteroatom dopant with a similar size to carbon atom and containing five valence electrons, easily donates electrons and changes the optical properties of CDs [36] to enhance the FLQY. A recent trend has emerged to prepare CDs from pollution-free carbon materials [30]. Meanwhile, CDs have high photoinduced electron transfer (PET) ability [31,37] and are good electron donors or receptors [38].

The supramolecular compound β‑cyclodextrin (β‑CD), a water-soluble, biocompatible, and nontoxic ring-shaped oligosaccharide, is polymerized from one 1,4‑glucosidic bond and 7 d‑pyran glucoses [39]. This unique configuration, which possesses both hydrophobic inner cavity and hydrophilic outer environment, can interact with different substances to form host–guest clathrates [40,41]. However, the substance entering the cavity of β‑CD cannot be qualitatively or quantitatively detected due to the chemical inertia of β‑CD. Thus, different β‑CD-functionalized fluorescent probes based on PET or the fluorescence resonance energy transfer can be manufactured by combining the high molecule recognizing ability and enantioselectivity of β‑CD with the excellent fluorescence properties of QDs [42,43].

Ferrocene (Fc) is a metal organic sandwich compound [44,45] commonly used in chemical detection owing to its outstanding electrochemical properties and stability [46,47]. When Fc as an electron donor [48] is close enough to CDs, effective excited-state electron transfer occurs, which significantly quenches the fluorescence [49]. Fc and its derivatives can hydrophobically interact with β‑CD to form stable 1:1 complexes [50,51]. After electron transfer in Fc, the loss of hydrophobicity weakens the complexation ability of Fc+; thus, Fc can be rapidly eliminated from the cavity and replaced by any substance that can form a 1:1 compound with β‑CD [52,53]. This property has been widely utilized into biochemical sensors [54,55].

In the present study, fish scales, a pollution-free domestic waste, were used as a carbon source to prepare CDs. The use of such an environmental-friendly, cheap, and accessible precursor material is very convenient for preparing water-soluble, low-toxicity, photostable and biocompatible CDs. N-doped CDs (β‑CD-CD-Fc+)-fluorescent sensors were functionalized by using the host-guest composite between mono(6‑amino‑6‑deoxy)‑β‑CD and (ferrocenylmethyl)trimethylammonium iodide (Fc+). When Fc+ entered the cavity of β‑CD, the distance between Fc+ and CDs was shortened, whereas the excited electrons on the CDs were transferred to the unoccupied molecular orbit of Fc+ and cannot return to the ground state, which induced the PET and reduced the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. β‑CD can recognize the guest CA [56,57], and CA competes with Fc+ for the cavity of β‑CD. Thus, CA can block the PET to restore the fluorescence of CDs. This study established a fast, safe and environmental-friendly fluorescence “switch” probe system with high sensitivity and selectivity.

The following chemicals, all of which were produced in China, were used in this study: mono(6‑amino‑6‑deoxy)‑β‑CD (98%, Shandong Binzhou Zhiyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd.); cholic acid, lithocholic acid (LCA), deoxycholic acid (DCA), chenodeoxycholic acid (CDCA) (all 98%), Fc+, (95%) (Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai); MgCl2 (98%), NaNO3, NaCl, KCl, urea, and glucose (all 99%) (Guangfu Chemical Company, Tianjin); glycine, ascorbic acid (both 99%), dl‑tryptophan, glutathione, and dl‑tyrosine (all 98%) (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai); and 1‑ethyl‑3‑(3‑(dimethylamino)propyl) carbodiimide (EDC HCl) and N‑hydroxysuccinimide ester (NHS) (both 98%; GL Biochem Ltd., Shanghai). Dialysis bags (molecular weight cutoff = 3500 Da) were supplied by Union Car-bide Co. (USA). The content of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was 10.0 mM. All reagents used in the experiments were analytically pure and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was generated by a Milli-Q purification system (US). Serum was purchased from Chongqing Manuik Technology Co. Ltd. (China).

Fluorescence was measureddetected on an LS-55 photoluminescence spectroscope (Perkin Elmer, USA) in quartz cuvettes (1.0 × 1.0 cm2) with the excitation and emission slit widths of 5 and 10 nm, respectively, and under excitation at 340 nm and, emission at 420 nm with the slit widths of 5 and 10 nm, respectively and quartz cuvettes (1.0 × 1.0 cm2). The samples were then tested under a Cary 300 ultraviolet-visibles (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Var-ian, USA) and a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM) instrument (FEI, USA) at acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Typically, each sample was prepared by dripping the sample solution onto the copper grids covered by porous amorphous carbon films. The samples were then characterized on a Niocolet 380 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer.

Nontoxic and environmental-friendly CDs were synthesized from a “top-down” one-step hydrothermal treatment of natural biomaterial fish scales and ultrapure water. Here, a hydrothermal method [32] with modifications was used. In brief, Ctenopharyngodon idellus was purchased from a local supermarket in Linfen City, Shanxi, China. The scales were scraped off, dried, and crushed into powder. Then, 1.5 g of the scale powder was mixed with 80 mL of ultrapure water under ultrasound. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluorethylene and then hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 20 h. The resulting brown dispersion was naturally cooled and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant in the brown dispersion was collected in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 3500 Da) and purified through dialysis in ultrapure water for 2 days. The resulting CD solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and then concentrated in a rotational evaporimeter at 50 °C into a brown thick slurry, which was then dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C. The experiment was performed at room temperature.

β‑CD-CDs nanoprobes were prepared through an existing method [33] with modifications. In specific, 0.5 mg·mL−1 CDs solution (30 mL) was added with 51 mg of NHS and 43 mg of EDC HCl under 1 h of complete stirring to activate the surface carboxyl groups of the CDs. Then, the CD mixed solution was added with 200 mg of mono(6‑amino‑6‑deoxy)‑β‑CD for 12 h of reaction. The resulting solution was removed to a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 3500 Da) and dialyzed for 24 h to remove excessive EDC HCl, NHS, unreacted β‑CD, and other by-products. Finally, the purified β‑CD-CD solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane and then vacuum-dried at 50 °C. The resulting 0.3 mg·mL−1 β‑CD-CDs solution, which was thick slurry and could adsorb water well, was used for further detection and characterization. The experiment was performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of β‑CD-CD-Fc+ host–guest fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: CA was detected via two phases of fluorescence quenching and recovery. In the first phase, 40 μg·mL−1 β‑CD-CDs, 10 mmol·L−1 PBS (pH 7.4), and Fc+ at different concentrations were mixed in 10 mL colorimetric tubes, which were all diluted with ultrapure water to 3 mL, shaken completely, and incubated at room temperature for 25 min to quench the fluorescence of β‑CD-CDs. The fluorescence intensity was measured at room temperature. The Fc+ concentration was optimized. In the second phase, β‑CD-CDs, 750 μmol·L−1 Fc+, and PBS were added into 10 mL colorimetric tubes under sufficient mixing and optimal conditions. After 25 min of incubation, different amounts of CA (0–1000 μmol·L−1) were added into the above mixed solutions under uniform shaking, and all solutions were diluted to 3 mL with ultrapure water. After 20 min of culture at room temperature, the optimization was conducted at the same excitation and emission conditions for the β‑CD-CDs-Fc+ probe construction. To avoid background interference, all experiments we adopted F/F0-1, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of the aptasensor with and without CA, respectively.

Urine (sampled from healthy adults) and serum samples were diluted 100 times in advance without further complex pretreatment to avoid the interference of the substrate. All samples were spiked with the same content of CA to test the recovery ability. The data were expressed as the mean of three measurements.

The β‑CD-CD nanoprobes were observed via FT-IR spectroscopy, TEM, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The surface carboxylic groups of the CDs were stimulated by EDC and NHS, and covalently bonded via CONH to β‑CD (Fig. 1a). The β‑CD-CDs showed three strong FT-IR peaks typical of β‑CD at 1155, 1079, and 1030 cm−1, which were ascribed to the COC asymmetric glucosidic bond vibration, CO, CC, and COH coupling tensile vibration. The strong stretching vibration peak of CO in the CDs shifted from 1652 to 1635 cm−1, and the peak of NH stretching of NH2 shifted from 3426 cm−1 to 3271 cm−1. The β‑CD-CDs showed the mixed characteristic FT-IR peaks of β‑CD and CDs, indicating that β‑CD and CDs were successfully connected via covalence to form an amide group (CONH), without the physical adsorption of β‑CD.

The UV–vis spectra clearly illustrate different absorption behaviors of CDs, β‑CD, and β‑CD-CDs (Fig. 1b). The UV absorption peak of the CDs at 270 nm was mainly ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic carbon ring [32]. After β‑CD was functionalized onto the CDs in the β‑CD-CDs nanoprobes, the n-π* transition occurring in the newly formed CONH led to two evident peaks at 230 and 300 nm, where the first peak was characteristic of the UV absorption by β‑CD. The β‑CD-CD nanoprobes in water solutions emitted slight blue fluorescence around 420 nm, with a blue shift of only 5 nm from the peak of the CDs (425 nm), but the excitation wavelength of β‑CD-CDs was 340 nm, with a blue shift of 20 nm from the peak of CDs. The blue shift of β‑CD-CDs in the fluorescence was attributed to the CONH formed between the surface carboxyl group of CDs and the amido group of β‑CD.

TEM images show that the sizes of the β‑CD-CDs nanoprobes fell within 2–5 nm (Fig. 1c,d), which is consistent with the size distribution of the original CDs. This result indicates that the new β‑CD-CDs nanoprobes did not alter the nanostructure of the CDs.
The IR and UV spectra suggested that CDs and β‑CD were well conjugated to form β‑CD–CD nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CA detection mechanism of the β‑CD-CD fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: Under the activation by the coupling agents EDC and NHS, the COOH of the CDs reacted with the -NH2 of β‑CD to form a CONH covalent bond. Thus, the CDs were bonded covalently onto β‑CD to form the β‑CD-CD fluorescent probes (Fig. 2 ). When the CDs were functionalized by β‑CD, the surfaces of the CDs also became hydrophobic [33]. Fc+ was compatible with and could quickly enter the cavity of β‑CD, forming a complex [50,51]. After the CDs were functionalized by β‑CD, Fc+ could also introduce the hydrophobic environment onto the surfaces of the CDs to shorten the surface distance between Fc+ and CDs. Thus, the electrons were photo-induced from the CDs, which acted as the electron donor of the fluorescent probes. The photo-induced excitation electrons under fluorescence irradiation were rapidly transferred to the unoccupied molecular orbit of Fe+ [33,49], quenching the fluorescence of the fluorescent probes, while PET occurred between the CDs and Fc+. When CA existed in the system, Fc+ competed with the CA molecules for host–guest in the holes of β‑CD and then removed from the cavity because the CA molecules could also enter the hole of β‑CD; in addition, the complexation between the Fc+ and β‑CD cavity was weakened after the electron transfer [52,53,58]. The removal of Fc+ from CA blocked the PET and restored the fluorescence of the β‑CD-CDs.

Due to the PET between β‑CD-CDs and Fc+, the fluorescence intensity of the β‑CD-CD probes decreased with increasing dosage of Fc+ within 0–750 μmol·L−1 (Fig. 3A). This result indicates that the fluorescence of the probes could be effectively quenched by Fc+. However, the fluorescence intensity of the probe reduced slowly when the Fc+ dosage was up to 750 μmol·L−1 and was nearly unchanged with further addition of Fc+. This result was probably because the β‑CD cavities on the β‑CD-CD probes were occupied by Fc+. Thus, the newly added Fc+ could not enter the cavities, thereby blocking the PET. To validate this statement, Fc+ (750 μmol·L−1) was added into a 40 μg·mL−1 CD solution and diluted to 3 mL (pH 7.4). Fc+ only slightly quenched the fluorescence of CDs compared with β‑CD-CDs. When 650 μmol·L−1 CA was added, the quenched fluorescence of the CDs could almost not be restored (Fig. A1 B). Although the CDs could induce PET with Fc+ to reduce the fluorescence of CDs, the CA could not block the PET between CDs and Fc+ if the hydrophobic cavities supplied by β‑CD for host–guest competition between CA and Fc+ were unavailable. Thus, the fluorescence intensity could not be recovered. Moreover, to validate the host-guest identification between Fc+ and cyclodextrin cavity, we added the same amount of CA (650 μmol·L−1) into both the β‑CD-CD solution and CD solution, but the fluorescence intensity did not change obviously. This fully suggests that CA and Fc+ competed for host-guest in the cavity of cyclodextrin and also confirms the host-guest identification action between Fc+ and cyclodextrin.

This result suggests that the β‑CD-functionalized CDs not only offered Fc+ with anchor points in the cavities of the host molecules but also accelerated the PET. In consideration that Fc+ could be removed by the host–guest competition of β‑CD, the PET was also blocked when Fc+ was eliminated by other molecules that could strongly bind with β‑CD, returning the electrons to the ground state and recovering the original fluorescence of the β‑CD-CDs. To avoid background interference throughout, we adopted F/F0-1, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities with and without CA, respectively.

As a latent biomarker of CA, pH is a major influence factor on system stability. Within the tested pH range (5, 6, 7, 7.4, 7.7, 8, and 8.96), the probe system performed the best at pH 7.4. The quenching time of Fc+ (Fig. 3B Time1) and the time of CA recovery were optimized (Fig. 3B Time2). The system quenching and recovery were optimized in the range 0–40 min (Fig. 3B). The quenching effect on Fc+ was optimized after 25 min (Time 1), whereas the recovery effect of CA was optimized after 20 min (Time 2) because the binding ability between CA and the β‑CD cavity was strengthened after the electron transfer. Thus, CA could more rapidly occupy the cavity and remove Fc+ from the cavity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, anti-interference, and stability of the β‑CD-CD-Fc+ fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the selectivity of the β‑CD-CD-Fc+ probe was tested by using the three major components of bile acid, including LCA, CDCA, and DCA. First, β‑CD-CDs (40 μg·mL−1), Fc+ (750 μmol·L−1), and PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) were mixed uniformly, diluted to 3 mL, and cultured for 25 min, fluorescence at which was set as blank. Then, the above mixture was separately added with the three acids (each 100 μmol·L−1). Cholic acid, LCA, CDCA, and DCA were set at the same concentration. A mixture containing the four substances was set as a control. All solutions were diluted to 3 mL. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate. The F/F0-1 of the system maximized at the dosage of 100 μmol·L−1 CA, but the fluorescence intensity only slightly increased from the blank control after the addition of other substances at the same dosage (Fig. 4A), indicating the high selectivity of this probe.

The interference from other potential coexisting substances in the serum (Na+, K+, Mg2+, NO3−, urea, glucose, glycin, DL-tryptophan, vitamin C, glutathione, and DL-tyrosine) on the biomarker role and CA detection of this probe was assessed. The system was not significantly affected after 500-fold dosage of Na+, K+, Mg2+, NO3−, or urea; 100-fold dosage of Glu, AA, or GSH; or 50-fold dosage of DL-Tyr, Gly, or DL-Trp was added into the probe system containing β‑CD-CDs, Fc+, and PBS (errors <5%; Fig. 4B). These results suggest that the other substances possibly existing in biological serum will not largely reduce the sensitivity of this probe. Thus, this probe may also be promising for biomedical practice and application. The stability of the β‑CD-CD probe was assessed by detecting the fluorescence intensity before and after placement in a freezer (4 °C) for 1 month. After the placement, the fluorescence intensity of the β‑CD-CD probe only declined by 5.77% (±0.02), which confirms the high stability of this probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CA detection by the fluorescent probe systems
ce:section.ce:para: The electron transfer between Fc and CDs reportedly leads to the hydrophobicity loss of the β‑CD cavity and to the weakened complexation ability of Fc+ [52,53,58]. When CA reached the cavity of β‑CD, Fc+ was rapidly expelled outside, which blocked the PET. Thus, the competition between CA and Fc+ for the cavity of β‑CD can be utilized to build β‑CD-CD nanoprobes and thereby detect CA. The fluorescence intensity variation after the addition of CA (0–650 μmol·L−1) was detected under optimal conditions (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescence intensity of the β‑CD-CD-Fc+ nanoprobes linearly correlated with the CA concentration within a certain range. The linear equation at the CA concentration 0–1 μmol·L−1 was F/F0-1 = 0.1292C[CA] + 0.0007 (R2 = 0.996). As the CA concentration in the detection system increased, the host–guest competition for Fc+ was decelerated, and a turning point appeared at 1 μmol·L−1 on the linear curve. The linear equation within a CA concentration of 1–650 μmol·L−1 was changed to F/F0-1 = 0.0005C[CA] + 0.1224 (R2 = 0.996), with the limit of detection (LOD) of 25 nmol·L−1 (RSD = 0.27, n = 11). LOD was calculated as LOD = 3 s/k from the first stage of the curve (0–1 μmol·L−1), where s is the standard deviation of 11 parallel measurements by the β‑CD-CD-Fc+ probe without addition of CA and k is the slope of the standard curve.

This new method was compared with other existing CA detection methods (Table A1). The detection limit of the new method was higher than those of LC-MS/MS [11], ultra-HPLC-MS/MS [10], HPLC-fluorescence detection [12], and pressurized capillary electrochromatography-laser-induced fluorescence [15]. Nevertheless, this method had a broader linear range and can be used to accurately detect CA above the normal level in the human body (<10 μmol·L−1) and thus identify hepatobiliary diseases. Despite its higher detection limit, this method had a narrower linear range than molecular imprinting [3], which is operationally complex and unable to rapidly detect CA. The new method had a narrower linear range but a much lower detection limit than the enzyme-linked method [18], indicating that the former is more sensitive. These findings prove that the β‑CD-CD-Fc+ fluorescent nanoprobe has high efficiency, environmental friendliness, and practicability for CA detection.

On the basis of the above results, the β‑CD-CD fluorescent probes were chosen for selective CA detection in human serum and urine. Under optimal conditions, CA (0.2, 0.5, or 1.0 μmol·L−1) was added to serum and urine samples to record the changing fluorescence intensity of the probe. The equation was calibrated to calculate the mean and spiked recovery within 97.1%–103.4% (error < 5%; Table 1 ). Results indicate that the β‑CD-CD fluorescence bioprobe can be applied, without any complex pretreatment, to detect hepatobiliary diseases in clinic.

N-doped carbon dots functionalized by β‑cyclodextrin were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal process. Based on the molecule recognizing ability of β‑cyclodextrin and the PET properties of CDs, we designed a sensitive and low-toxicity fluorescence nanobioprobe with high cell compatibility, photostability, and anti-interference ability. This probe for CA detection in human serum and urine yielded a detection limit of 25 nmol·L−1 and two linear ranges of 0–1 and 1–650 μmol·L−1. This probe is safe, reliable, sensitive, and fast without any complex pretreatment. This study expands the clinical medicine research and theoretically underlies the biomedicine development.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.03.066.
